title: Installing stock netting for sheep and goats
subtitle: The fence that contains the animals that electric won't.
excerpt: Electric fencing works well for pigs, cattle, and managed poultry, but sheep and goats (particularly determined goats) routinely push through or duck under a single electric wire. Stock netting (woven wire with graduated spacing) is the proven containment for small ruminants. Installing it correctly means understanding straining posts, tensioning, and the stapling sequence.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef & Lamb fencing guidance, ahdb.org.uk. DEFRA Farm fencing and livestock management guidance, gov.uk. Farming Connect Wales, practical fencing guide.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fence post placed at the end of a fence run and at all corners, set deeper and at a backward angle to resist the tension of the taut wire. The end post bears all the longitudinal stress; intermediate posts only bear lateral pressure from animals.

Woven wire fence with rectangular apertures, graduated from small at the bottom (to prevent lamb heads getting stuck) to larger at the top. UK standard sizes include C8/80/15 (cattle and sheep, 80 cm high, 15 cm stays) and L6/90/15 (high tensile, 90 cm).

content.text: comes in 50-metre rolls. For sheep, the 90 cm-high C8/80/15 or L6/90/15 grade is standard in the UK. For goats, go to 100 cm height and consider adding a top strand of plain wire or electric wire, as goats routinely lean over a net and press it down.

at each end of the fence run and at corners, angled slightly backward (away from the direction the wire will pull) using a post rammer. Straining posts for a 50-metre net run of sheep netting should be at least 150 mm diameter treated softwood, set 750 mm deep. Brace each straining post with a diagonal brace post and tie-wire to the base of the post behind it; without bracing, even a short run of taut netting will pull the straining post forward within a season.

Drive intermediate posts at 3-metre intervals for sheep netting and 2.5-metre intervals for areas with heavier animal pressure. Use a string line between the end posts to keep the run straight. Intermediate posts can be lighter than straining posts (75 mm diameter, 600 mm deep) as they do not bear longitudinal tension.

Attach the beginning of the roll to the first straining post and walk the roll out along the fence line. Before stapling, tension the netting along the run using a fencing bar or a long length of timber as a lever hooked through the squares of the net. The net should be taut enough to spring back when pushed but not so tight it distorts the aperture pattern. Staple to intermediate posts once tensioned, starting in the middle of the run and working outward to both ends.

The bottom horizontal wire of the net must sit close to the ground to prevent lambs crawling under. On uneven ground, pin the net to the soil contour using metal U-pins (fencing staples driven horizontally) between posts, or pack any gap with a stone. Check the fence for bright cut wire ends at joins and bend them flat so no animal injures an eye or a leg on a protruding wire.
